text: The surgeon prepares to perform a limited right hemicolectomy to surgically remove the polyp located in the ascending colon.
The surgeon prepares to perform a limited right hemicolectomy using a single incision technique, focusing on the ascending colon where the polyp is located.
The surgeon makes a four-centimeter incision around the patient's belly button as part of the single incision technique for the limited right hemicolectomy.
The surgeon enters the abdominal cavity through the previously made incision around the belly button, initiating the surgical access for the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon inserts a port into the abdominal cavity through the single incision made around the belly button, preparing for the limited right hemicolectomy.
The camera view focuses on the right side of the abdomen, where the limited right hemicolectomy is being performed.
The surgeon identifies the ink mark on the colon, indicating the location of the polyp within the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon visually identifies the cecum and the small intestine within the abdominal cavity, noting the absence of significant adhesions between these structures.
The surgeon reflects the transverse colon upwards, revealing underlying structures in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon exposes the duodenum, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon searches for the vessel supplying the right colon, focusing on identifying its location within the surgical field.
The surgeon retracts the cecum, revealing a suspending vessel targeted for dissection.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and a vessel that is tented up, providing a clear view of the anatomical structures.
The surgeon begins by opening the peritoneum beneath the vessel using an articulating end seal device, preparing for work on the flexures.
The surgeon creates a small window underneath the vessel feeding the right colon, providing a clear view of the anatomical structure.
The surgeon continues to dissect the peritoneum underneath the mesentery, creating a small space and proceeding with a hand-over-hand technique to further separate the tissue layers.

The surgeon maintains the space created earlier, using one hand to lift and gently sweep the tissue, gradually moving the duodenum away from the dissection area. The duodenum is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon observes the duodenum descending along with small vessels, all situated in the retroperitoneal space. The peritoneum is being carefully dissected over the top of these structures.
The surgeon identifies a vessel clearly visible beneath the dissected peritoneum, indicating its location within the surgical field.
The surgeon displays the ligated ileocolic artery and the resulting stump, highlighting the completion of the vessel ligation step in the procedure.
The surgeon observes the pulsating stump of the ligated ileocolic artery, with the duodenum visible below and safely positioned.
The surgeon lifts the tissue adjacent to the duodenum, continuing dissection in the retroperitoneal plane, aiming to separate and bring down the retroperitoneal tissue.
The surgeon sweeps the retroperitoneal tissue down, maintaining the dissection plane adjacent to the duodenum.
The surgeon adjusts the position by moving hand over hand to apply tension on the tissue, preparing for further manipulation in the surgical field.
The surgeon uses tension to sweep down the retroperitoneal tissue, revealing the colon. The visibility of the colon indicates sufficient progress in the dissection.
The surgeon begins to remove some of the insignificant fibers and proceeds to work on the lateral part of the surgical field.
The surgeon completes the medial mobilization of the colon, ensuring the retroperitoneal tissue is adequately swept down and separated.
The surgeon mobilizes the small bowel off the pelvic brim after ligating the vessel and dissecting the retroperitoneum, ensuring clear separation of the bowel from surrounding structures.
In the right lower quadrant, the surgeon views the small intestine while applying tension with the left hand. The ureter is visible running beneath the tissue being manipulated.
The surgeon points out a visible line marking the boundary between the dissected retroperitoneal area and the colon.
The surgeon crosses instruments during single-incision laparoscopic surgery, a technique necessitated by limited angles available in this approach.

The surgeon continues to mobilize the right colon, applying tension to reveal the undersurface of the retroperitoneum. The medial plane has not yet been reached, providing a clear view of the retroperitoneal structures.
